subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10. In this unit you will become confident and fluent with percentages – one of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics. A percentage is simply a way of describing a part of something out of one hundred, and once you see how it works you will notice percentages everywhere. When Amina compares two market stalls in Hargeisa to find the bigger discount, when Yusuf works out how much interest his savings will earn, or when Halima checks whether a trader made a fair profit selling goats, they are all using percentages.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult.

Before we begin, let us remind ourselves of the foundation. A percentage means per hundred – the word literally breaks into *per* (for every) and *cent* (hundred), the same *cent* you find in *century* (100 years). So 25% means 25 out of every 100. You already know how to swap between percentages, decimals and fractions: 25% = 0.25 = 1/4. We divide by 100 to turn a percentage into a decimal, and multiply by 100 to go the other way. Keep this at your fingertips, because every method in this unit rests on it.

As you work, keep a pencil, paper, and if you have one a calculator beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Write out each step, check that your answers make sense, and if you make a mistake, treat it as a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Calculate a percentage of an amount quickly using decimals, fractions and mental methods.
Find a percentage change using the formula change ÷ original × 100%.
Increase or decrease an amount using a multiplier (for example ×1.15 or ×0.85).
Solve reverse percentage problems to find the original amount before a change.
Calculate simple interest using Interest = Principal × Rate × Time.
Work out profit and loss as a percentage of the cost price.
Apply percentages to real problems: discounts, tax, tips, savings and trade.

concepts.title: Finding a Percentage of an Amount
concepts.explanation: Everything in this unit builds on one core skill: finding a percentage of an amount. There are three reliable methods, and a strong mathematician chooses the quickest one for each problem. Let us look at all three, because being able to switch between them is what makes you fast and accurate. Method 1 – the decimal method. Turn the percentage into a decimal by dividing by 100, then multiply. To find 30% of 80: 30% = 0.30, and 0.30 × 80 = 24. To find 15% of 240: 15% = 0.15, and 0.15 × 240 = 36. This method works…
concepts.example: Method 2 – the fraction method. Some percentages are friendly fractions. Because 25% = 1/4, finding 25% of 120 is just 120 ÷ 4 = 30. Because 50% = 1/2, we halve; because 10% = 1/10, we divide by 10. To find 60% of 150,…

concepts.title: Percentage Increase and Decrease
concepts.explanation: Very often a quantity does not stay still – it goes up or down. A price rises, a population grows, a discount lowers a cost. To find the new amount, you have two good methods, and it is worth knowing both. Method A – find the change, then add or subtract. To increase $60 by 25%: first find 25% of $60 = 0.25 × 60 = $15, then add it on: $60 + $15 = $75. To decrease 80 kg by 15%: find 15% of 80 = 12 kg, then subtract: 80 − 12 = 68 kg. This method shows clearly *how much* the change is, which is helpful when a problem…
concepts.example: Method B – use a multiplier. This is faster and is the professional way to do it. A multiplier is a single decimal that does the whole job in one multiplication. For an increase, you multiply by (100% + increase),…

concepts.title: Calculating Percentage Change
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you are told the old value and the new value, and you must work out the percentage change between them. There is one formula for this, and it is worth learning by heart: Percentage change = (change ÷ original amount) × 100%
concepts.example: The two things to be careful about are: first, the change is the difference between the new and old values; and second, you always divide by the original (the starting value), never by the new one. Choosing the wrong…

concepts.title: Reverse Percentage Problems
concepts.explanation: This is one of the most powerful ideas in the unit, and one that trips up many learners – so read it slowly. In a reverse percentage problem you are told the amount *after* a change, and you must find the original amount from before the change. The secret is to think in multipliers and then divide by the multiplier instead of multiplying. Here is the key idea. Going forwards, we do: original × multiplier = new amount. So going backwards we must do the opposite of multiplying, which is dividing: original = new…
concepts.example: Example – after an increase. After a 20% pay rise, Omar's monthly salary is now $36,000. What was it before? A 20% increase uses the multiplier 1.2, so original × 1.2 = 36,000. To reverse it, divide: original = 36,000 ÷…

concepts.title: Simple Interest
concepts.explanation: When you save money in a bank, the bank pays you extra money for keeping it there – this reward is called interest. When you borrow money, you pay interest instead. In this unit we study simple interest, which is the most straightforward kind: the interest is worked out only on the original amount you saved, called the principal, and it is the same every year. The formula is short and worth memorising:
concepts.example: Interest = Principal × Rate × Time, often written I = P × R × T.

concepts.title: Profit and Loss
concepts.explanation: Trade is at the heart of daily life, from the camel markets of Garissa to the shops of Eastleigh. Anyone who buys goods to sell them again needs to understand profit and loss. The cost price is what an item cost to buy or make; the selling price is what it is sold for. If the selling price is higher, there is a profit; if it is lower, there is a loss. To express profit or loss as a percentage, we use our percentage-change idea, and by convention we compare against the cost price:
concepts.example: Percentage profit = (profit ÷ cost price) × 100%, and likewise for loss.

methods.title: How to increase or decrease by a percentage
steps: Decide if it is an increase (add) or a decrease (subtract).
Write the multiplier: 1 + p for an increase, 1 − p for a decrease.
Multiply the amount by the multiplier.
Check the answer is larger (increase) or smaller (decrease) than you started with.

methods.title: How to find a percentage change
steps: Find the change: new value − old value.
Divide the change by the ORIGINAL value.
Multiply by 100% to turn it into a percentage.

methods.title: How to solve a reverse percentage
steps: Write the multiplier for the change that happened.
Set up: original × multiplier = new amount.
Divide the new amount by the multiplier to find the original.
Check by working forwards again.

methods.title: Percentage change
methods.example: A shopkeeper's weekly revenue rises from $800,000 to $920,000. Find the percentage increase.
steps: Original = 800,000.
Remember to divide by the original, 800,000.

methods.title: Reverse percentage
methods.example: After a 15% discount, a television costs $340. What was the original price?
steps: Solution: A 15% discount uses the multiplier 0.85, so original × 0.85 = 340.
Reverse it: original = 340 ÷ 0.85 = $400.
Check forwards: $400 × 0.85 = $340.
Correct.

methods.title: Simple interest
methods.example: Zahra invests a principal of $500 at 4% simple interest for 2 years. Find the interest and the total amount.
steps: Solution: Interest = P × R × T = 500 × 0.04 × 2 = $40.
Total amount = principal + interest = 500 + 40 = $540.
Check the simple interest result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Percentage of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use the decimal method. 12% = 0.12, so 0.12 × 250 = 30 shillings. As a check with building blocks: 10% of 250 = 25, and 2% is one fifth of that, 5, giving 25 + 5 = 30. Both agree.

workedExamples.title: Increase with a multiplier
workedExamples.solution: Solution: An 8% increase uses the multiplier 1.08. So $450 × 1.08 = $486. Check by parts: 8% of 450 = 36, and 450 + 36 = 486. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Decrease with a multiplier
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A 12% decrease uses the multiplier 0.88 (because 100% − 12% = 88%). So 350 × 0.88 = 308 kg. Check by parts: 12% of 350 = 42, and 350 − 42 = 308. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Percentage change
workedExamples.prompt: A shopkeeper's weekly revenue rises from $800,000 to $920,000. Find the percentage increase.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change = 920,000 − 800,000 = 120,000. Original = 800,000. Percentage increase = (120,000 ÷ 800,000) × 100% = 0.15 × 100% = 15%. Remember to divide by the original, 800,000.

workedExamples.title: Reverse percentage
workedExamples.prompt: After a 15% discount, a television costs $340. What was the original price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A 15% discount uses the multiplier 0.85, so original × 0.85 = 340. Reverse it: original = 340 ÷ 0.85 = $400. Check forwards: $400 × 0.85 = $340. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Simple interest
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra invests a principal of $500 at 4% simple interest for 2 years. Find the interest and the total amount.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Interest = P × R × T = 500 × 0.04 × 2 = $40. Total amount = principal + interest = 500 + 40 = $540.

workedExamples.title: Percentage profit
workedExamples.prompt: Idris buys a goat for $40 and sells it for $52. Find the percentage profit.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Profit = 52 − 40 = $12. Percentage profit = (profit ÷ cost price) × 100% = (12 ÷ 40) × 100% = 30%. We divide by the cost price, 40.

workedExamples.title: Combined discount and tax
workedExamples.prompt: A jacket costs $120. A 25% discount is applied, then 8% tax is added on the discounted price. Find the final cost.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Discount multiplier 0.75, tax multiplier 1.08. Final cost = 120 × 0.75 × 1.08 = 90 × 1.08 = $97.20. Doing both steps as one line of multipliers is quick and reliable.

workedExamples.title: Reverse from a total
workedExamples.prompt: A savings account grows to $920 after one year of simple interest at 15%. What was the principal?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: One year at 15% multiplies the principal by 1.15 (the principal is kept and 15% interest is added). So principal × 1.15 = 920, giving principal = 920 ÷ 1.15 = $800. Check: $800 interest at 15% = $120, and 800 + 120 = $920. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A trader buys goods for $50 and sells for $65. Find the percentage profit.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A restaurant bill is $65 with an 18% tip. Find the total using a multiplier.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: After a 20% increase, a salary is $48,000. Find the original salary.

activities.title: Market Discount Detective
activities.materials: a notebook, a pen, and some real shop or market prices (from a visit, a receipt, or an advertisement).
steps: Collect five items with their original prices and a discount percentage for each.
For each item, work out the multiplier (for example, 30% off means ×0.70).
Calculate the sale price and the money saved for every item.
Rank the deals from the biggest saving to the smallest. Which discount saved the most shillings?
Write one sentence explaining why a bigger percentage does not always mean a bigger saving in shillings.

activities.title: The Savings Growth Game
steps: Imagine you save a principal of $1,000 at 5% simple interest.
Make a table with columns for Year, Interest that year, and Total so far.
Fill in the total for years 1 to 5. With simple interest, the same interest is added each year.
Now try a rate of 8% and compare. How much more do you earn over five years?
Discuss with your family: why does a higher interest rate matter so much for long-term saving?

activities.title: Family Trade Investigation
activities.materials: a family member who buys and sells anything (food, livestock, goods), or an imagined trade.
steps: Choose an item with a cost price and a selling price (for example, a goat bought for $40 and sold for $52).
Work out the profit or loss in dollars or shillings.
Turn it into a percentage profit or loss on the cost price.
Try three different selling prices and see how the percentage profit changes.
Decide the lowest selling price that still gives at least a 20% profit.

activities.title: Percentage Claim Fact-Check
activities.materials: a newspaper, a radio advert you remember, or a product label.
steps: Find three claims that use a percentage, such as 50% more free or prices cut by 30%.
For each claim, work out what the number really means with an example amount.
Ask: is the claim clear, or could it mislead a shopper? What is missing?
Write a short consumer tip explaining how to check a percentage claim before believing it.
Being able to question percentage claims protects you and your family from being misled.

activities.title: Reverse Percentage Puzzle Maker
activities.materials: paper and pen, and a partner to challenge.
steps: Secretly choose an original price and a percentage discount.
Work out the sale price using the multiplier.
Give your partner only the sale price and the discount, and ask them to find the original.
Check their answer against your secret original. Swap roles and repeat.
Make the puzzles harder by using a discount and then a tax, both as multipliers.

activities.title: Budget Percentages (Family Task)
activities.materials: an imagined monthly income, such as $2,000, and paper.
steps: Split the income into percentages: for example housing 30%, food 20%, transport 15%, savings 10%, other 25%.
Check your percentages add up to 100%.
Work out the amount in dollars for each part of the budget.
Set aside the savings portion and calculate one year of simple interest on it at 5%.
Discuss with your family which percentages you might change, and why.

terms: A part of something out of 100
The decimal you multiply by to make a change
Finding the original from the final amount
The starting amount saved or borrowed
Interest paid only on the principal
What an item cost / what it sold for
Selling price minus cost / cost minus selling price
commonMistakes: Dividing by the new value in a change
Always divide by the ORIGINAL amount
Taking the % off the final amount

The decimal you multiply by to make a change
Finding the original from the final amount

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult.

I can before we begin, let us remind ourselves of the foundation. A percentage means per hundred – the word literally breaks into *per* (for every) and *cent* (hundred), the same *cent* you find in *century* (100 years). So 25% means 25 out of every 100. You already know how to swap between percentages, decimals and fractions: 25% = 0.25 = 1/4. We divide by 100 to turn a percentage into a decimal, and multiply by 100 to go the other way. Keep this at your fingertips, because every method in this unit rests on it.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, paper, and if you have one a calculator beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Write out each step, check that your answers make sense, and if you make a mistake, treat it as a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can calculate a percentage of an amount quickly using decimals, fractions and mental methods.
I can find a percentage change using the formula change ÷ original × 100%.
I can increase or decrease an amount using a multiplier (for example ×1.15 or ×0.85).
I can solve reverse percentage problems to find the original amount before a change.
I can calculate simple interest using Interest = Principal × Rate × Time.
